claims.text: Levent Alpöge — an Anthropic researcher — announced on X that the Jacobian conjecture is false, crediting mathematician Akhil Mathew for posing the question that reopened the search and Claude Fable 5, Anthropic's model, for the construction work done "during the World Cup final"
The map is explicit: F: C³ → C³, built from degree-7 polynomials, with Jacobian determinant identically equal to −2
three distinct points feed into the same output: F(0, 0, −1/4), F(1, −3/2, 13/2), and F(−1, 3/2, 13/2) all map to (−1/4, 0, 0)
hello there the jacobian conjecture is false thanx to my close friend akhil for asking about it and my other close friend fable for working during the world cup final
Mathematician David Speyer reconstructed the map from the posted coordinates and verified the collision and the constant Jacobian on his own blog within hours of the announcement

he holds a 2020 Princeton PhD supervised by Fields Medalist Manjul Bhargava, was a Junior Fellow at Harvard's Society of Fellows, and won the 2015 Morgan Prize, the top undergraduate mathematics award in the United States
The Bass–Connell–Wright reduction of 1982, building on Yagzhev's 1980 work, showed that the full conjecture (for any n) follows from a special "cubic-homogeneous" sub-case
MathWorld and other survey sources count at least five published incorrect proofs of the Jacobian conjecture, plus many more incorrect attempts that never reached print
in 2004, Carolyn Dean circulated a proof that was initially endorsed by commutative-algebra expert Mel Hochster, only for a gap to surface and the endorsement to be retracted
In 2009, Dang Vu Giang posted a purported proof to arXiv and, after being shown the flaw, acknowledged it himself
A pull request — #4474 — was opened against Google DeepMind's Formal Conjectures GitHub repository to add a machine-checked Lean version of the disproof

The Lean-formalized version circulating in the pull request used a rescaled counterexample with Jacobian determinant 1, not Alpöge's original −2 ... contributor "deancureton" acknowledged that the attribution "isn't exactly correct"

a 2026 historical study by Lázaro O. Rodríguez Díaz, published in the peer-reviewed journal Comptes Rendus Mathématique, argues that the precise two-variable statement now called the Jacobian conjecture actually appears first in an 1884 paper by German mathematician Ludwig Kraus, "Über Functionaldeterminanten" — 55 years before Keller
As of this writing, the MacTutor History of Mathematics archive — a standard reference for exactly this kind of dating question — makes no mention of Kraus at all

When the model launched on June 9, 2026, the company's own materials framed it around software engineering — a Stripe migration case study and a top score on Cognition's "FrontierCode" eval — agentic knowledge work (a leading result on Hebbia's Finance Benchmark) ... Mathematics research appeared nowhere in that positioning

the US government ordered Anthropic to suspend all Fable 5 and Mythos 5 access for any foreign national, anywhere, including the company's own foreign-national employees — and Anthropic's official X account posted about the suspension directly and promptly
Fable 5 came back globally, but Mythos 5 was restored only for a vetted set of US organizations the government had approved on June 26

Google DeepMind's AlphaProof and AlphaGeometry 2, which scored a silver-medal-equivalent 28 out of 42 at the 2024 International Mathematical Olympiad using Lean formal proof-checking, peer-reviewed and published in Nature

Gemini Deep Think pushed further along the autonomy axis a year later, reaching full gold-medal-standard IMO performance in 2025 — 35 out of 42, five of six problems solved — entirely in natural language, with no Lean formalization step at all
in May 2026, OpenAI announced — with three named mathematician endorsements — that a model had disproved an approximately 80-year-old planar unit-distance conjecture

October 2025, when an OpenAI VP publicly claimed GPT-5 had "solved" ten previously unsolved Erdős problems; the claim collapsed within days after the Erdős-problems curator showed the model had merely surfaced already-published solutions
Terence Tao's judgment that an April 2026 GPT-5.4-Pro-assisted closure of a 1968 Erdős problem was genuine, "Book-proof"-quality insight

Aaron Lou, a researcher at OpenAI, has stated that an internal, non-web-search version of an OpenAI reasoning model independently derived "essentially the same" counterexample without first having seen Alpöge's announcement

CoinDesk ran a piece amounting to "Claude's Fable 5 just solved an 87-year-old math problem, and it matters for bitcoin"; ... its case is that continued AI capability progress "strengthens the case" for speculative capital rotating out of crypto and into AI/compute infrastructure
On July 21, 2026 — the day after the announcement — bitcoin rallied to roughly $66,000–$66,500, a ~5-week high and about a 3% move in 24 hours

Financial press attributed that rally to five consecutive days of spot-ETF inflows exceeding $700M and optimism around pending crypto-market-structure legislation, and noted it moved together with the Nasdaq-100 — a risk-on co-movement
